title: Mohinga
titleMy: မုန့်ဟင်းခါး
desc: Burma's national dish — a rich, aromatic fish broth poured over rice vermicelli. Every family has their own treasured variation.
culturalNote: Burma's national dish. Eaten for breakfast on every street corner but also at any hour — the rules are loose. Every family has their own version, passed down however it got passed down. The broth is the thing: silky, thickened with rice flour, built around fish that should be gone by the time you taste it but still present in every spoonful.

steps.body: Simmer the fish with crushed lemongrass, turmeric, and 500ml water for 12–15 minutes. The fish should be opaque but not falling apart — pull it just before you think it's done. Reserve the broth (about 500ml), flake the flesh carefully discarding all bones and skin, and discard the lemongrass.

Blend the onion, garlic, ginger, soaked chillies, and shrimp paste into a rough paste. Chunky is better than smooth — a mortar and pestle keeps things coarse, which means the paste cooks unevenly in the best way.

Heat oil in a large pot and fry the paste on medium for 5–6 minutes, stirring constantly. You want it to go from bright and raw-smelling to amber-brown and deeply fragrant. Raw garlic in the broth is the most common mistake — properly cooked garlic gives depth.
Add the flaked fish to the fried paste and mash it in gently over medium heat. Stir in the paprika. This is where the broth's flavor base gets built. Cook for 2 minutes, stirring gently.
Pour in the reserved broth plus the remaining 1 litre of water and bring to a gentle simmer. Keep it gentle. A rolling boil turns the broth murky and the texture suffers.
Mix the toasted rice flour with a cold ladle of broth in a separate bowl until smooth — no lumps. Pour it slowly into the simmering broth while stirring. It thickens fast.
Season with fish sauce and black pepper. Simmer on low for 8–10 minutes. The broth should coat the back of a spoon — not thick like gravy, but not watery either. Taste it and fix what's off.

Cook the rice vermicelli per packet and drain well. Put noodles in warmed bowls, ladle broth over (about 250ml per person), and add whatever garnishes you're using: halved boiled egg, fresh coriander, crispy fried onions, lime wedges. The lime gets squeezed at the table.

title: Shan Noodles
desc: Noodle dish from Shan State with layered toppings. Garlic oil, chilli, and peanuts all go in separately at the table.
culturalNote: From the Shan people of Eastern Burma, now eaten everywhere. The point is the layering — garlic oil, chilli, peanuts all go in separately at the table, not pre-mixed in the kitchen. Each person builds their own bowl. That tableside control is not incidental; it's the whole idea.

steps.body: Split the garlic: mince 4 cloves for the sauce, slice the other 6 thinly. Fry the sliced garlic in 3 tbsp oil over medium heat, stirring constantly, for 2–3 minutes until light golden. The window between golden and burnt is about 30 seconds — watch it. Pull with a slotted spoon. That oil is now the base of everything.

In the same garlic oil over medium-high, add the chicken in a single layer. Leave it alone for 2 minutes. Undisturbed contact with the hot pan builds a crust and keeps the juices in. Once browned on both sides (about 4 minutes total), push the chicken to one side.

Add the minced garlic, chopped tomatoes, and diced shallots to the empty space. Cook for 3–4 minutes until the tomatoes fully break down and the mixture turns jammy. The tomato acidity is what cuts through the fat.
Add the chilli flakes, soy sauce, sugar, salt, and five spice. Stir and simmer for 2–3 minutes. Smell it — if it's flat, add soy; if it's too sharp, a pinch of sugar rounds it out.
Cook the rice noodles per packet. They should be springy, not soft. Drain and rinse under cool water to stop the cooking.

Divide noodles into bowls, top with the chicken sauce (about 4 tbsp per bowl). At the table, each person drizzles in their own garlic oil, sweet soy sauce, extra chilli flakes, and crushed peanuts, then toss together. This final assembly is not optional — it's the soul of the dish.

title: Burmese Fish Curry
titleMy: ငါးဟင်း
desc: Simple tomato-based curry with turmeric-marinated fish. Made in homes across the country from Yangon to riverside villages.
culturalNote: Fish is central to Burmese cooking, especially in coastal and riverside regions. This simple tomato-based curry is made in homes across the country. The turmeric marinade is the signature element: it creates a protective golden crust that keeps the flesh moist during cooking and contributes an earthy depth that no other spice can replicate.

steps.body: Rub the cubed fish gently with the salt and turmeric powder, coating every surface. Set aside for 10 minutes to marinate. The turmeric acts as both a flavor penetrant and a protective barrier: it draws out minor impurities from the fish and creates a thin crust on the surface.

Heat the oil in a heavy-bottomed pan over medium heat. Add the chopped shallots and fry, stirring frequently, until they turn soft and deeply golden (about 4 minutes). This slow golden cooking is the flavor foundation — they should smell sweet and caramelized.

Add the grated garlic and cook for 30 seconds, stirring constantly — garlic burns in seconds. Add the chilli powder and turmeric, bloom them in the hot oil for 30 seconds, then immediately add the chopped tomatoes and a small pinch of salt.

Pour in the warm water slowly and bring to a gentle simmer. The broth should be fragrant and slightly oily, glowing orange-gold from the spices. Once simmering, carefully lay the marinated fish pieces into the broth without stirring.

Cook on low-medium heat for 2–3 minutes, then use a spoon to gently turn each piece of fish, supporting it from beneath. Continue cooking covered for another 2–3 minutes until the fish is just cooked through.
Add the fresh chillies, fish sauce, and coriander. Stir very gently — think of moving the curry, not stirring it — and cook uncovered for 1–2 more minutes. Serve with steamed jasmine rice.

title: Laphet Thoke
titleMy: လက်ဖက်သုပ်
desc: Pickled tea leaf salad served at the end of every Burmese meal. Historically a peace offering between warring kingdoms.
culturalNote: Historically a peace offering between warring kingdoms, laphet (pickled tea) was exchanged as a symbol of reconciliation. Today it is served at the end of every Burmese meal and at all social gatherings — weddings, funerals, business meetings. It is arguably the most culturally significant dish in Myanmar.

steps.body: Dress the shredded lettuce very lightly with fish sauce — just enough to season it, not so much that you can taste it separately. The fish sauce adds background depth and draws a little moisture from the lettuce. Arrange the dressed lettuce on a large serving plate or traditional lacquered platter.

In a bowl, combine the pickled tea leaves with minced garlic, grated ginger, finely chopped green chillies, lemon juice, and oil. Mix very thoroughly with a fork or your fingers so every tea leaf is evenly coated.

Taste the dressing carefully and adjust. You are aiming for a four-way balance: astringent from the tea, sour from the lemon, salty from fish sauce or salt, and spicy from chilli. Each element should be present but none should dominate.
Add the halved cherry tomatoes around or on top of the lettuce. Place the laphet tea dressing in the center of the plate in a mound.

Arrange all the crunchy toppings — peanuts, sesame seeds, fried split peas, dried shrimp — in separate distinct piles around the edges of the plate, so each element is visible and diners can see what they're choosing.

At the table, squeeze lime juice over everything. Then toss the salad together gently using two large spoons or clean hands, mixing just enough to distribute the dressing without pulverizing the toppings. Each person adjusts the ratio in their individual bowl.

title: Ohn No Khao Swè
desc: Mandalay specialty with curry noodles and coconut milk. Reflecting centuries of cultural exchange along trade routes.
culturalNote: A beloved Mandalay specialty reflecting centuries of cultural exchange along the Mughal trade routes connecting India and Southeast Asia. The dish carries the warm, fragrant stamp of Indian spicing — curry powder, turmeric, chickpea flour — filtered through a distinctly Burmese sensibility: rich but not heavy, coconut-sweet but savory.

steps.body: Toast the chickpea flour in a dry pan over medium heat, stirring constantly for 3–4 minutes until it turns pale golden and smells nutty and fragrant — like warm toasted grain. Remove immediately from heat; toasted chickpea flour thickens the broth and adds a subtle earthiness that no cornstarch substitute can replicate.

Blend or pound the onions, garlic, and ginger into a rough paste. Heat the oil in a large heavy-bottomed pot and fry this paste over medium heat for 5–6 minutes, stirring constantly. Watch for the transformation: it will sizzle and steam initially, then gradually turn from pale to golden-amber.

Add the curry powder, turmeric, and chilli powder directly to the hot paste and stir vigorously for 1 minute to bloom the spices in the oil. The mixture will smell intensely aromatic and turn a deep golden-orange. Add the chicken thighs and stir to coat every surface in the spiced paste.

Pour in the coconut milk and chicken stock, stirring to lift any aromatics stuck to the bottom of the pot. Bring to a gentle bubble over medium heat — you want a lazy simmer, not a rolling boil. A rolling boil will cause the coconut milk to split.

Reduce heat to medium-low and cover. Simmer for 20–25 minutes until the chicken is very tender. Remove the chicken pieces and shred the meat off the bones, discarding the skin. Return the shredded meat to the pot.

Whisk the toasted chickpea flour with a ladle of warm broth in a small bowl until completely smooth — lumps here will remain lumps in the finished curry. Pour this slurry slowly into the simmering broth while stirring constantly. The broth will thicken visibly over the next 2 minutes.

Add the fish sauce and taste carefully. The finished broth should be rich and coconutty, gently spiced, savory, and slightly sweet. Cook noodles per packet directions. Serve in deep bowls: noodles first, then broth ladled over, then toppings arranged separately.

title: Mont Lin Ma Yar
titleMy: မုန့်လင်းမယား
desc: Beloved street food snack sold by vendors with cast iron takoyaki-style pans. The name means 'husband and wife'.
culturalNote: A beloved street food snack sold by vendors with cast iron takoyaki-style pans filled with small round wells, often set up at dawn alongside tea stalls. The name translates to 'husband and wife' — the egg yolk nestled in the center of each ball is said to resemble a couple sitting close together. A quintessential Burmese childhood memory.

steps.body: Combine the rice flour, all-purpose flour, baking powder, salt, and sugar in a large bowl and whisk the dry ingredients together thoroughly. Gradually add the coconut milk while whisking, pouring in a thin stream to prevent lumps from forming. The batter should be smooth and thick — similar to cake batter — not as thin as pancake batter.

Heat a takoyaki pan, aebleskiver pan, or small muffin tin over medium heat for 1–2 minutes until thoroughly hot. Brush each well generously with neutral oil, coating the bottom and sides completely. A properly oiled, properly hot pan is the secret to the crispy exterior.

Fill each well halfway with batter using a small ladle or spoon. Immediately crack one quail egg into the center of each well, being careful not to break the yolk. Add a few slices of spring onion and a small pinch of dried shrimp on top of the egg.

Spoon more batter over each well until nearly full, leaving just a thin sliver of space at the top. Cook on medium heat for 3–4 minutes. Watch the edges: when the batter pulls slightly away from the pan wall and turns light golden, the bottom has set and it is time to flip.

Using a long skewer or takoyaki pick, insert the tip down the side of one ball and begin rotating it smoothly in a 180-degree arc. The ball should release cleanly from the pan and land with its raw batter side down. Cook the second side for 2–3 minutes until golden and set.

Remove with a skewer or slotted spoon and serve immediately with sweet chilli sauce for dipping. Mont lin ma yar must be eaten hot — within 5 minutes of cooking — while the exterior is still crispy and the quail egg inside is still custardy. They lose their magic quickly as they cool.

title: Shwe Gyi Sanwin Makin
titleMy: ရွှေကြည်ဆနွင်းမကင်း
desc: Golden semolina cake made for celebrations and merit-making ceremonies. Popular during Thingyan, the Burmese New Year.
culturalNote: A golden, fragrant semolina cake made for celebrations, merit-making ceremonies at Buddhist pagodas, and Thingyan — the joyful Water Festival that marks the Burmese New Year. The name roughly translates to 'golden clarified semolina cake.' The signature topping of black poppy seeds and white sesame seeds creates a speckled crown that is immediately recognizable at any Burmese celebration table.

steps.body: Preheat your oven to 180°C (350°F). Grease a 20cm square baking tin generously with butter or oil, coating both the base and all four sides — the cake's high egg and sugar content makes it prone to sticking.

Place the coarse semolina in a large, heavy-bottomed pan over medium heat. Toast it dry, stirring constantly with a wooden spoon, for 5–7 minutes. At first it will smell raw and floury; gradually it will shift to a warm, nutty, popcorn-like aroma and the color will darken from pale cream to pale gold.

In a separate saucepan, combine the coconut milk and water and bring to a gentle simmer over medium heat, stirring occasionally. The liquid must be genuinely hot — near-boiling — before you pour it over the semolina.

Pour the hot coconut milk mixture over the toasted semolina in a steady stream, stirring vigorously and constantly. The semolina will absorb the liquid rapidly and the mixture will thicken within 30 seconds. Add the sugar, butter or ghee, cardamom powder, and a pinch of salt. Stir until fully combined and the butter has melted completely.

Beat the 3 eggs well in a separate bowl until the yolks and whites are fully combined. Once the semolina mixture has cooled enough that it does not steam aggressively (it will still be warm — that is fine), pour in the beaten eggs and stir quickly and thoroughly.

Pour the batter into the prepared tin and smooth the top with a wet spatula — the dampness prevents sticking. Sprinkle the poppy seeds and sesame seeds evenly over the entire surface in a single generous layer.

Bake for 30–35 minutes until a skewer inserted into the center comes out completely clean and the top is deep golden-brown. Cool completely in the tin for at least 1 hour before cutting — the cake continues to set as it cools. Cut into 2–3cm squares and serve at room temperature.

title: Spicy Chicken Fried Rice
titleMy: ကြက်သားဆီထမင်း
desc: Everyday home dish distinguished by bold use of fish sauce, garlic, and chilli. Made with day-old rice and whatever protein is on hand.
culturalNote: Burmese fried rice is distinguished from its Chinese cousin by its bold, unapologetic use of fish sauce, garlic, and chilli — simpler in construction but deeply intense in flavor. It is an everyday home dish, often made to use day-old rice (which fries better than fresh) and whatever protein is on hand.

steps.body: Heat a wok or large skillet over high heat until it begins to smoke slightly — this high heat is non-negotiable for good fried rice. Add 1 tbsp oil, crack in the eggs, and scramble them quickly, breaking them into rough bite-sized pieces. Remove the cooked egg to a side plate immediately; it will finish cooking with residual heat.

Return the wok to high heat and add the remaining 1 tbsp oil. Once the oil shimmers, add the minced garlic and sliced shallots and stir-fry for 30–45 seconds until fragrant and just beginning to turn golden. The aromatics should sizzle vigorously and perfume the oil — they should not sit and steam.

Add the sliced chicken and stir-fry for 2–3 minutes, tossing constantly, until cooked through and no longer pink inside. Burmese fried rice chicken should be tender, not rubbery — high heat and constant movement keeps it moist.

Add the day-old rice (cold rice is essential — freshly cooked hot rice releases steam and becomes sticky). Break up any clumps immediately with the side of a metal spatula or wok turner as you toss. Stir-fry for 2–3 minutes, tossing frequently so every grain gets coated in the fragrant oil.

Pour the fish sauce and soy sauce around the edges of the wok rather than directly onto the rice — the liquid hits the hot pan first and vaporizes, infusing the rice with flavor rather than making it wet. Add the chilli powder and toss continuously for 30–45 seconds.

Return the scrambled egg to the wok, add the chopped spring onions, and toss everything together for another 15–20 seconds until the onions just begin to wilt — they should still be bright green. Transfer immediately to a plate and serve hot. Fried rice waits for no one.

title: Nan Gyi Thoke
titleMy: နန်းကြီးသုပ်
desc: Beloved Burmese noodle salad eaten as a hearty breakfast. Thick rice noodles tossed with fragrant chicken curry sauce.
culturalNote: A beloved Burmese noodle salad eaten especially as a hearty breakfast or brunch. The thick, round rice noodles (nan gyi, meaning 'large tubes') are tossed warm with a fragrant chicken curry sauce, garlic oil, and aromatic toppings. Like mohinga, it is fundamentally a breakfast-counter dish: sold at tea shops and street stalls, eaten quickly but with pleasure.

steps.body: Heat the oil in a heavy-bottomed pan over medium heat. Add the finely sliced onion and fry, stirring regularly, until soft and lightly golden at the edges, about 5–6 minutes. The onion must be patient-cooked here — it is the sweetness foundation of the curry sauce.

Add the minced garlic and ginger and sauté for 1–2 minutes, stirring constantly, until fragrant and light gold. Add the turmeric, chilli powder, and paprika directly to the aromatics and stir vigorously for 30 seconds to bloom the spices in the hot oil.

Add the chicken pieces and stir to coat completely in the spiced oil. Allow the chicken to sit undisturbed for 2 minutes to develop a light golden crust on one side, then stir and cook for another 2 minutes.

Season with fish sauce, sugar, salt, and a small splash of water — about 3 tbsp. Stir to combine, then cover the pan and reduce heat to low-medium. Simmer for 15–20 minutes until the chicken is cooked through and very tender.

Remove the chicken and shred it into rough pieces, discarding any bones. Return the shredded chicken to the pan and cook uncovered for 2–3 more minutes, stirring, so the sauce reduces further and coats every piece of meat.

Cook the thick rice noodles according to packet directions. Rinse them briefly under cool water to stop the cooking, then drain very well — excess water dilutes the sauce. Toss the warm noodles with the chicken curry mixture and a drizzle of extra oil.

Serve immediately in deep bowls, topped with thinly sliced spring onions and a generous squeeze of fresh lime. The lime is not optional — its acidity cuts through the richness of the curry and brightens every element of the dish.

title: Tohu Thoke
titleMy: တိုဟူးသုပ်
desc: Shan tofu salad with chickpea flour-based tofu. Light but deeply satisfying with bright lime-garlic dressing.
culturalNote: Shan tofu salad (tohu thoke) is a classic expression of Shan cuisine's elegant simplicity. The tofu is made from chickpea flour rather than soy — it has a custard-like, golden texture quite unlike anything in Chinese or Japanese cooking. Common in hot weather because it is light but deeply satisfying, its bright lime-garlic dressing cutting cleanly through the gentle richness of the tofu.

steps.body: Whisk the chickpea flour, turmeric, and water together in a large bowl until completely smooth — take your time and eliminate every lump before you start cooking, because lumps that enter the pot will remain in the finished tofu. The turmeric turns the mixture a vibrant yellow. Let the mixture rest for 5 minutes so any air bubbles can rise to the surface.

Pour the mixture into a heavy-bottomed pot over medium-low heat. Begin stirring immediately with a wooden spoon, scraping the bottom and sides of the pot in a continuous, deliberate motion. After 5 minutes, the mixture will begin to thicken and become creamy; after 10 minutes it will visibly pull away from the sides.
Continue cooking and stirring for a total of 12–15 minutes until the mixture moves as a thick, glossy mass rather than a liquid.

Immediately pour the hot tofu into a shallow dish or baking pan, spreading it quickly and evenly to a uniform 1cm thickness. Work fast — the tofu begins to set as soon as it leaves the heat. Smooth the surface with a wet spatula. Let it cool to room temperature, about 30 minutes.

Refrigerate the cooled tofu for at least 2 hours, preferably overnight. This chilling step is essential and cannot be skipped — the tofu needs time in the cold to develop its characteristic firm-but-custardy texture.

While the tofu chills, make the dressing: mince the garlic very finely and combine it with the lime juice, fish sauce, chilli flakes, and neutral oil. Stir well; the oil will partly emulsify with the lime juice, creating a loose, bright dressing.

Cut the firm chilled tofu into 2cm cubes. In a large bowl, combine the tofu with the shredded cabbage, fresh coriander leaves, and coarsely crushed roasted peanuts. Pour the dressing over and toss gently — the tofu is delicate. Serve immediately while the tofu is cold and the cabbage is crisp.

title: Burmese Tofu
desc: Yellow chickpea tofu foundational to Shan cuisine. Used in salads, fried as a snack, and served alongside soups.
culturalNote: This yellow chickpea tofu is a foundational ingredient in Shan cuisine, used daily in salads, fried as a snack, and served alongside soups and rice. Unlike soy tofu, which is pressed and firm, Shan tofu is cooked from a starch suspension — closer in spirit to polenta or congee than to anything derived from curdled milk.

steps.body: In a large bowl or measuring jug, whisk together the chickpea flour, turmeric, and salt. Gradually add the water in a thin stream while whisking continuously — adding water too quickly causes the flour to clump before it can disperse. The finished mixture should be completely smooth, the color of turmeric-gold, and pourable.

Pour the mixture into a heavy-bottomed pot and place over medium-low heat. Begin stirring immediately with a wooden spoon, making long sweeping strokes across the bottom and up the sides of the pot. After about 5 minutes, the mixture will begin to thicken and change color from pale yellow to a deeper, warmer gold.

Continue cooking and stirring for 15–20 minutes total. The key sensory signal is when the mixture stops flowing and starts moving as a cohesive, thick mass — it should pull away cleanly from the sides as you stir and leave a brief trail on the surface.

Working quickly, pour the hot tofu into a shallow 20cm square or round dish, spreading it to an even 1cm thickness with a wet spatula. The tofu begins to set the moment it leaves the heat, so speed matters. Let it cool completely at room temperature (about 30 minutes).

Refrigerate for at least 2 hours, or overnight. The cold firms the tofu substantially — it will be much easier to cut and will hold its shape far better after proper chilling. Do not rush this step by putting it in the freezer, which would damage the delicate starch structure.

To fry: cut into rectangular pieces about 1cm thick and 5cm long, pat them dry with paper towels, and lower them into oil heated to 175°C. Fry for 2–3 minutes per side until the exterior is light golden-brown and crispy. The contrast between the golden, slightly crunchy exterior and the custardy interior is what makes Burmese fried tofu so appealing.

title: Htamin Jin
titleMy: ထမင်းချဉ်
desc: Distinctive Intha and Shan specialty rice salad from Inle Lake region. Warm rice and potato tossed in garlic-infused oil.
culturalNote: A distinctive Intha and Shan specialty from the lake region around Inle Lake in Shan State. It represents a Burmese approach to the salad concept: not cold, not dressed in Western vinaigrette, but warm and earthy — cooked rice and potato tossed in garlic-infused oil, finished with raw tomato for freshness and fish sauce for depth.

steps.body: Heat the oil in a heavy-bottomed pot or wok over medium heat. Add the minced garlic and fry, stirring frequently, for 2–3 minutes until it turns light golden and smells deeply fragrant — not bitter, not raw. At this stage, the oil has absorbed the garlic's flavor completely.

Add the diced boiled potatoes to the garlic oil and stir gently for 1–2 minutes. The potatoes will absorb the garlic-infused oil and their surfaces will take on a faint golden sheen. They add earthiness and body to what would otherwise be a very light dish.

Add the cooked rice, breaking up any clumps gently with a fork or the back of a wooden spoon as you stir. The goal is individual grains of rice, each coated in garlic oil — not a sticky mass. Day-old rice works best. Stir and toss for 2–3 minutes.

Remove the pot from heat and stir in the diced tomatoes. Adding them off the heat is deliberate: the tomatoes should remain chunky and fresh, providing bursts of acidity rather than cooking down into the background. Pour in the fish sauce.

Taste carefully and adjust the seasoning. The salad should be savory and aromatic from garlic oil, with a clean freshness from the raw tomato, a pleasant saltiness from fish sauce, and the gentle earthiness of potato and rice beneath everything.

Transfer to a serving platter or individual bowls. Scatter fresh coriander leaves or thinly sliced green onion over the top if using. Htamin jin is served at room temperature or slightly warm — never piping hot and never cold from the fridge.

title: Vegetarian Mohinga
titleMy: သက်သတ်လွတ် မုန့်ဟင်းခါး
desc: Compassionate adaptation of Burma's national dish honoring Buddhist vegetarian principles. Mushrooms and lemongrass replace fish.
culturalNote: A compassionate adaptation of Burma's national dish that honors Buddhist vegetarian principles deeply embedded in Burmese monastic and lay life. Many Burmese monasteries and vegetarian-focused restaurants in Yangon serve this version, particularly during Buddhist observance days (Uposatha days) when devout Buddhists abstain from meat.

steps.body: Bring the 1.5 litres of water to a vigorous boil in a large pot. Add the crushed lemongrass stalks, sliced ginger, crushed garlic cloves, halved onion, and turmeric. These aromatics are doing the work that fish normally does in traditional mohinga — they must infuse the water with maximum flavor. Reduce to a steady simmer.

Simmer the aromatics for 15 minutes, maintaining a gentle but active bubble throughout. The water should have turned golden from the turmeric and smell intensely of lemongrass and ginger. Strain the broth through a fine sieve into a clean pot, pressing the aromatics firmly with a spoon to extract every drop of infused liquid.

Return the strained broth to medium heat and bring back to a simmer. Add the sliced mushrooms — use a mix of varieties if possible. Cook for 5–7 minutes until the mushrooms are tender and have released their savory juices into the broth.

Mix the toasted rice flour with a ladle of cool (not hot) broth in a separate bowl, whisking until completely smooth and lump-free. This tempering step prevents the flour from clumping. Pour this slurry slowly into the simmering broth while stirring constantly.

Season generously with soy sauce and salt. Simmer for 8–10 minutes on low heat, stirring occasionally, until the broth is velvety and coats the back of a spoon lightly. Taste and adjust: the broth should be savory, fragrant with lemongrass, gently earthy from mushrooms and rice flour.

Cook the rice vermicelli separately per packet directions and drain very well. Divide into warmed serving bowls. Ladle the hot broth generously over the noodles (about 250ml per serving) and finish with traditional garnishes: halved boiled eggs, crispy fried shallots, fresh coriander, thinly sliced spring onions, chilli flakes, and lime wedges.

title: Chicken Curry with Potatoes
titleMy: ကြက်သားအာလူးဟင်း
desc: Classic Burmese home curry in 'sibyan' style where oil separates and rises to the surface, indicating perfect balance.
culturalNote: A classic Burmese home curry representing the 'sibyan' style of cooking — the definitive technique of Burmese curries, in which the oil separates and rises to the surface of the dish after the spices have bloomed and the moisture has cooked off. This separation of oil is not a flaw but a sign of mastery, indicating the aromatics are fully cooked.

steps.body: Heat the oil in a heavy-bottomed pot over medium heat. Add the thinly sliced onions and fry, stirring every minute or so, for 5–6 minutes until they are soft, translucent, and lightly golden at the edges. The onions are the flavor foundation of this curry — patient cooking develops their natural sugars and creates a sweet, mellow base.

Add the minced garlic and cook for 1–2 minutes, stirring constantly, until fragrant and just beginning to color. Sprinkle the turmeric evenly over the onion-garlic mixture and stir for 30 seconds to bloom it in the hot oil — this releases turmeric's essential oils and transforms its flavor.

Add the chicken chunks in a single layer if possible and stir to coat every piece completely in the turmeric-spiced oil. Allow the chicken to sit undisturbed for 2–3 minutes so the surfaces make good contact with the hot pan and develop a light golden crust.

Pour in enough water to half-cover the chicken pieces (about 500ml). Stir to lift any aromatics stuck to the bottom. Bring to a boil over medium-high heat, then immediately reduce to medium-low. Cover partially and simmer for 15–20 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the chicken is cooked through and tender.

Add the potato chunks and increase the heat slightly to medium. Cook uncovered, stirring gently every few minutes, for 15–20 minutes until the potatoes are completely tender. As the curry simmers, watch the surface carefully: the oil will begin to separate from the broth and collect in pools on top.

When sibyan appears — a thin, distinct layer of oil clearly separate from the sauce beneath — the aromatics are fully cooked and the curry has reached its ideal flavor state. Taste and adjust the salt. The sauce should be thick enough to coat the back of a spoon lightly. Serve hot alongside steamed jasmine rice.

title: Burmese Chicken Soup
titleMy: ကြက်သားဟင်းချို
desc: Clear, light home-style soup served as component of traditional Burmese rice meal. A cleansing and sustaining note.
culturalNote: A clear, light home-style soup served as one component of the traditional Burmese rice meal alongside curries, a salad, and fermented condiments. This style of broth is called 'hin gyo' — a 'sweet soup' or 'clear broth' — and its purpose is deeply intentional: it cleanses the palate between bites of richly spiced curry and aids digestion.

steps.body: Place the chicken pieces — bone-in pieces are strongly preferred here, as bones release collagen and marrow into the broth, creating a silky body that boneless chicken cannot achieve — in a large pot along with the halved onion, crushed garlic cloves, and sliced ginger. Add 1 litre of cold water, then bring to a boil over high heat.

As soon as the water reaches a boil, begin skimming the grey-white foam that rises to the surface. This foam is denatured protein and impurities released by the chicken — removing it is what gives hin gyo its characteristic clean, pure taste and pale clarity. Skim patiently for 8–10 minutes.

Reduce the heat to medium-low and simmer gently, barely covered, for 30–40 minutes. The surface should barely tremble — a hard boil would make the broth cloudy and give it a harsher flavor. The chicken is ready when the meat is very tender and separates easily from the bone.

Taste the broth and season very lightly — hin gyo is intentionally subtle. A few drops of fish sauce add background umami without making it taste 'fishy'; plain salt keeps it clean and neutral. The goal is a broth that tastes of chicken, ginger, and simplicity.

Ladle into small individual bowls. Scatter a few leaves of fresh coriander over each bowl and add a grind of black pepper. Serve alongside — not before — the rest of the Burmese meal. Diners sip from the bowl between bites of rice and curry, allowing the clear broth to refresh the palate.

title: Moh Let Saung
titleMy: မုန့်လက်ဆောင်း
desc: Chilled dessert drink with sago pearls, coconut milk, and jaggery syrup. Beloved street stall treat during hot season.
culturalNote: A chilled Burmese dessert drink made with sago pearls, coconut milk, and jaggery syrup — a beloved street stall treat consumed in enormous quantities during the hot season, when temperatures in Yangon regularly exceed 40°C. Sold from pushcarts and market stalls in large bowls over crushed ice.

steps.body: Bring 1 litre of water to a full rolling boil in a large pot. Add the sago pearls in a thin, steady stream while stirring constantly — adding them all at once causes them to clump together in an irreversible mass. Continue stirring for the first 2 minutes to keep each pearl separate as they begin to soften.

Reduce heat to medium and cook for 15–20 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the sago pearls are almost completely translucent. Hold one against the light: it should be clear around the edges with just a tiny white star-like dot visible at the center. Do not overcook — fully clear sago becomes mushy.

Pour the cooked sago into a fine sieve or colander and rinse under cool running water, gently stirring the pearls with your hand to wash away the excess starch. This rinsing step is essential: without it, the pearls will stick together as they cool.

Make the jaggery syrup: break the jaggery into rough pieces and combine with 250ml water in a small saucepan. Bring to a boil over medium heat, stirring until every piece dissolves. The syrup will be a warm, dark amber color that smells of caramel and toasted palm. Simmer for 2–3 minutes until slightly thickened, then cool to room temperature.

In a large bowl or pitcher, gently combine the cooled sago pearls, jaggery syrup, and full-fat coconut milk. Stir with a light hand — you want everything evenly mixed without breaking the pearls. The mixture should look like pearls suspended in a caramel-tinged coconut cream.

To serve: fill glasses or deep bowls halfway with the sago-coconut mixture. Pile crushed ice generously on top. Drizzle any remaining syrup over the ice for extra sweetness. Serve immediately with a spoon and encourage diners to stir the melting ice into the coconut cream, thinning and chilling the mixture as they eat.

title: Banana Fritters
desc: Common Myanmar street snack often sold with sweet Burmese milk tea at dawn. Coconut milk batter sets them apart.
culturalNote: A common Myanmar street snack, often sold alongside savory fritters and cups of sweet Burmese milk tea at dawn. The vendor's cast-iron griddle, slicked with oil and set up at the side of a busy road or market entrance, is one of the most familiar sights of a Burmese morning. These fritters are a childhood staple — simple, inexpensive, instantly recognizable.

steps.body: Whisk together the rice flour, sugar, salt, and baking powder in a large bowl until evenly combined. Gradually pour in the coconut milk while whisking continuously, adding it in a thin stream to prevent lumps. The batter should be thick enough to cling to a banana slice when dipped — similar to a thick pancake batter.

Peel the bananas and slice them diagonally at about 1cm thickness. Diagonal cuts create oval slices with more surface area than straight rounds, which means more batter contact and a crispier exterior per bite. Choose bananas that are ripe but still firm.

Pour oil into a wok, deep fryer, or heavy-bottomed pan to a depth of at least 3cm and heat to 170–180°C. Test the temperature by dropping a small piece of batter into the oil — it should sizzle immediately, rise to the surface within 2 seconds, and turn pale golden within 30 seconds.

Working in small batches of 4–5 slices to avoid crowding (crowding drops the oil temperature and produces greasy, soggy fritters), dip each banana slice into the batter to coat it completely. Let any excess batter drip off for a second, then carefully lower each slice into the hot oil. Lay it away from you to prevent splashing.

Fry for 2–3 minutes on the first side until light golden-brown, then gently turn each fritter with tongs or a slotted spoon and fry for another 1–2 minutes on the second side. The finished fritter should be golden, not deep brown — dark brown means the batter has cooked past crispy into bitter.

Remove with a slotted spoon and drain on paper towels. Banana fritters should be served within 10–15 minutes of frying, while the batter is still crispy and the banana inside is hot and soft. As they cool they lose their textural contrast rapidly. Serve with sweet chilli sauce or a cup of strong Burmese tea.

title: Fried Tofu with Tamarind Sauce
desc: Fried tofu snacks with tamarind sauce. The contrast of crispy, golden tofu against tangy sauce expresses Shan cooking's genius.
culturalNote: Fried tofu snacks are common in Shan and Burmese street food culture, sold at market stalls and tea shops alongside other fried things. Tamarind adds a sweet-sour balance — simultaneously tangy and caramel-tinged — that works brilliantly for diaspora kitchens where distinctly Burmese ingredients may be hard to find.

steps.body: Cut the tofu into rectangular pieces about 1cm thick and 5cm long — this shape maximizes surface area in the oil while keeping the interior thick enough to stay custardy rather than cooking through entirely. Pat each piece firmly dry with paper towels; surface moisture is the enemy of a good crust, causing the tofu to steam rather than fry.

Pour the oil into a wok or heavy-bottomed pan to a depth of at least 2cm and heat over medium-high until it reaches 170–180°C. To test without a thermometer, drop a small piece of tofu into the oil — it should sizzle energetically and rise to the surface immediately.

Working in batches to avoid crowding the pan (crowding causes the oil temperature to drop, producing greasy results), carefully lower the tofu pieces into the hot oil one at a time. Do not move them for the first 1–2 minutes — this allows a golden crust to form. After 2–3 minutes total, use tongs to turn each piece gently and fry the other side for 1–2 minutes until light golden-brown all over.

Remove with a slotted spoon and drain on paper towels. The exterior should be lightly crisp and golden; the interior should remain soft and custardy. Serve within 5–10 minutes of frying — tofu loses its crispness as it cools.

Make the tamarind sauce while the tofu fries: heat 1 tsp oil in a small saucepan over low heat, add the minced garlic, and fry for 1 minute until fragrant and just beginning to soften — do not let it brown. Add the tamarind paste, sugar, and water, stirring to dissolve. Bring to a very gentle simmer.

Simmer the sauce for 3–4 minutes, stirring occasionally, until it is slightly reduced, glossy, and pourable. It should taste intensely sour-sweet with a background of garlic. Add the fish sauce and stir to incorporate — it adds the savory umami depth that transforms the sauce from sweet-sour into something complex and rounded. Taste and adjust.

Arrange the fried tofu on a plate and serve the warm tamarind sauce in a small bowl alongside for dipping. Each piece of warm, crispy tofu dipped into the tangy sauce should deliver the full flavor contrast simultaneously — hot and cool, crispy and saucy, savory and sharp.

title: Samosa Salad
titleMy: ဆာမိုဆာသုပ်
desc: Popular street-food style salad reflecting Burmese snack culture and Indian influence. Breaking samosas into a salad transforms elements.
culturalNote: A popular street-food style salad that reflects both Burmese snack culture and the deep Indian influence in Burmese cuisine — the samosa itself arrived via the Indian traders and merchants who settled in Burma for centuries. Breaking perfectly good samosas into a salad might seem counterintuitive, but it is quintessentially Burmese.

steps.body: Prepare the vegetables first: shred the cabbage into very fine ribbons using a sharp knife or mandoline — fine shreds absorb the dressing better and integrate more smoothly with the samosa pieces than thick chunks would. Dice the tomato into 1cm pieces, removing and discarding the watery core. Slice the red onion into very thin half-moons.

Make the dressing in a small bowl: combine the chilli sauce, tamarind paste, a pinch of salt, and 1 tbsp water. Stir until the tamarind dissolves completely and the sauce becomes smooth and pourable. Taste it carefully — it should be intensely sour, spicy, and slightly sweet, with enough body to coat the salad components.

Break the samosas by hand into rough, irregular chunks about 2–3cm in size. Breaking by hand rather than cutting with a knife creates jagged edges with more surface area that will catch and hold the dressing. The pieces should be substantial enough to retain some crunch.

Immediately before serving — not a moment before — combine the shredded cabbage, tomato, sliced onion, and broken samosa pieces in a large bowl. Pour the dressing over everything at once and toss gently but thoroughly using your hands or two large spoons, mixing for about 1 minute. The goal is to coat every component evenly while keeping the samosa pieces intact enough that they still provide crunch.
Taste once assembled and adjust: if the salad needs more acidity, squeeze lime over; if it needs more heat, add chilli flakes. Transfer immediately to a serving plate or individual bowls.

Scatter fresh coriander leaves or torn mint over the top right at the table — the herbs should be added at the last possible moment so they remain vibrant and aromatic rather than wilting under the dressing. Samosa salad must be eaten immediately; after 10 minutes the samosa pieces absorb the dressing and lose their crispness.

title: Bean Curry with Rice
titleMy: ပဲဟင်း
desc: Simple, nourishing everyday Burmese home dish. Humble ingredients elevated by patience and proper technique.
culturalNote: A simple, nourishing everyday Burmese home dish made when meat is unavailable or when families want a lighter meal. It represents the mild, sustaining side of Burmese home cooking — humble ingredients elevated by patience and proper technique. The simplicity of this curry is intentional: it is meant to support and accompany a bowl of rice without overwhelming it.

steps.body: If using dried beans, soak them in cold water overnight (at least 8 hours). Drain, rinse, and boil in fresh unsalted water for 45 minutes to 1 hour until completely tender but not falling apart — they should hold their shape but give easily when pressed between two fingers. If using tinned beans, drain and rinse them thoroughly and proceed directly to the next step.

Heat the oil in a heavy-bottomed pot over medium heat. Add the thinly sliced onion and fry, stirring every minute or so, for 4–5 minutes until soft, translucent, and just beginning to turn golden at the edges. Onions are the flavor foundation of this curry — patient cooking over medium heat develops their natural sweetness and creates a mellow, rounded base. Rushing this step produces a sharp, raw-tasting result.

Add the minced garlic and stir for 1 minute until fragrant. Sprinkle the turmeric directly over the onion-garlic mixture and stir vigorously for 30 seconds to bloom it in the hot oil — this transforms the raw, slightly harsh turmeric into a warm, mellow, rounded spice. The oil will turn deep golden-yellow.

Add the cooked beans and stir gently to coat them in the spiced oil, turning them over for about 1 minute. The beans will absorb the turmeric color and begin to smell aromatic. Pour in 500ml of water (or light vegetable stock for more depth) and stir to combine.

Bring to a simmer over medium heat, then reduce to medium-low and cook uncovered, stirring gently every few minutes, for 15–20 minutes. The curry should reduce and thicken gradually as it cooks. Watch that the beans remain intact — they should soften slightly and absorb flavor without breaking down into a paste.

Taste the finished curry and season with salt to prefermence. The curry should be mild and slightly earthy, with a gentle savory warmth from the turmeric and a soft sweetness from the onion — no single flavor element should stand out aggressively. This is a dish meant to harmonize with rice, not compete with it. Serve hot alongside steamed jasmine rice.
